blocks.text: Chapter 7.
Rethinking at Scale: Charged Topics and Classrooms
The ideas in 30 seconds
Binary bias — our habit of collapsing complex issues into two warring camps — hardens positions, while presenting the full range of perspectives (complexifying) opens people up to rethinking.

In Peter Coleman's Difficult Conversations Lab, pairs who read a "many-sided" version of an article before discussing abortion had markedly more constructive conversations than pairs who read the standard "two-sided" version.

Climate communication fails when it addresses only believers and deniers; Yale's research identifies six distinct audiences, and most persuadable people sit in the middle segments that binary framing erases.

Caveats, contingencies, and admissions of uncertainty do not weaken an argument — for skeptical audiences they increase credibility and persuasion, and mixed emotions signal deeper thinking rather than weakness.
Erin McCarthy teaches history by having students critique outdated textbooks and rewrite incomplete chapters, training them to treat knowledge as provisional rather than fixed.

Louis Deslauriers's Harvard experiment showed students in active-learning classes scored higher but felt they learned less than lecture students — fluent teaching creates an illusion of learning that effortful rethinking punctures.
Binary Bias: Why Two Sides Are Worse Than Many

Psychologists Matthew Fisher and Frank Keil documented binary bias: when we face a complex spectrum of evidence or opinion, we simplify it into two categories — for or against, us or them. The simplification feels clarifying, but it destroys the middle ground where rethinking happens. Once an issue has exactly two sides, holding your side becomes an identity contest rather than a search for truth.

The antidote is complexifying: deliberately surfacing the many angles, sub-questions, and internal disagreements inside a supposedly two-sided issue. Complexity doesn't muddy a debate; it signals that the question is genuinely open, which licenses people to be curious instead of defensive. Even journalism's norm of "balance" backfires here — presenting one scientist against one contrarian implies a 50/50 split where a 97/3 consensus actually exists, a distortion called false balance.

Columbia psychologist Peter Coleman tested this in his Difficult Conversations Lab, where strangers who disagree on incendiary topics try to reach enough common ground to write a joint statement. Before pairs discussed abortion, Coleman had them read a news article on another polarizing issue: gun control. One version framed it as a two-sided war; the other covered the same facts but presented gun control as a multi-dimensional problem with many perspectives and mixed views. Pairs primed with the complexified article went on to have visibly better conversations — more curiosity, less entrenchment, and a far better shot at producing a statement both could sign.

Coleman also found a signature in the transcripts: productive conversations contained a wider, more variable emotional range — positive and negative feelings intermingled — while failed conversations locked into a narrow loop of frustration. Emotional ambivalence, feeling two things at once, isn't fence-sitting; it's evidence that someone is actually processing complexity. The practical move for anyone writing or arguing about a hot topic: don't present two poles. Present the spectrum, the trade-offs, and the points where informed people disagree with their own side.
Six Climate Audiences, Not Two

Climate discourse is binary bias at civilizational scale: believers versus deniers. But the Yale Program on Climate Change Communication finds six distinct audiences in the U.S. — the alarmed, concerned, cautious, disengaged, doubtful, and dismissive. Only a small fraction, roughly one in ten Americans, are truly dismissive. The rest occupy a middle that binary messaging ignores or insults.

This matters because rhetoric calibrated for deniers alienates the cautious and disengaged, who don't reject climate science — they just haven't prioritized it or don't know what to do. Treating them as the enemy pushes them toward the pole you fear. Even vocabulary carries the bias: lumping the doubtful in as "deniers" misses that skepticism is a scientific virtue — being persuadable by evidence — while denial is rejecting evidence regardless. Calling a genuinely doubtful person a denier converts a potential rethinking conversation into an identity attack.

Grant also notes that dramatic, one-sided advocacy — the An Inconvenient Truth model — energizes the alarmed but hardens everyone else. Documentaries and op-eds that acknowledge complexity, uncertainty ranges, and legitimate disagreements about solutions (not the basic physics) reach the persuadable middle better than apocalyptic certainty. And rather than perspective-taking — imagining what skeptics think, which research shows we do poorly, especially across ideological gaps — Grant recommends perspective-seeking: asking people directly about their views and the experiences behind them. You cannot simulate a worldview you've never inhabited; you can only inquire into it.
The Persuasive Power of Caveats and Calibrated Uncertainty

Communicators fear that admitting uncertainty kills credibility. The evidence says the opposite, at least with skeptical audiences. Studies of expert testimony and science communication find that when a source acknowledges limits — "this finding may not generalize," "the effect sizes vary" — audiences rate the source as more trustworthy and are more persuaded, because hedged claims sound like honest reporting rather than salesmanship. Confident certainty on a contested topic reads as preaching; calibrated uncertainty reads as science.

Grant learned this personally after publishing a hard-line critique of emotional intelligence that provoked furious backlash. When he rewrote his case with caveats and contingencies — where emotional intelligence matters (emotionally demanding work) and where it doesn't — the nuanced version invited engagement instead of war. The lesson: caveats aren't concessions that dilute your argument; they're complexity markers that flag you as a thinker rather than a partisan. (This differs from the argument-dilution problem in chapter 5 — that was about stacking many weak reasons; caveats are honest boundary conditions on strong ones.)

The same logic applies to forecasts and public claims. Sources that express probability ranges rather than certainties keep their credibility when reality surprises everyone, and they model the rethinking mindset for their audience. On charged topics, the most persuasive stance is usually: here is what we know, here is what we don't, and here is where reasonable people still disagree.
Erin McCarthy: Teaching Students to Interrogate the Textbook

Rethinking can be taught, and eighth-grade history teacher Erin McCarthy — Wisconsin's 2020 Teacher of the Year — shows how. She opens units by assigning excerpts from a 1940 history textbook. Students initially absorb it as authoritative; then they discover its glaring omissions and distortions, and the shock recalibrates them. The point isn't that old books were wrong and new ones are right — it's that all codified knowledge is a snapshot, written by particular people at a particular time, subject to revision.

McCarthy then makes revision the students' job. Her classes investigate the people and perspectives their current textbooks underplay — she has students research overlooked historical figures and write their own chapters, effectively rewriting the textbook rather than memorizing it. Instead of learning history as settled fact, they learn it as an ongoing argument they can join. That reframe converts passive consumers of knowledge into active fact-checkers.

The broader principle: schools mostly reward having answers, which trains confidence in conclusions; rethinking requires confidence in the process of questioning. Grant argues that lessons should be structured so students regularly encounter credible information that contradicts what they were just taught, learn that textbooks disagree with each other, and practice updating. Related classroom evidence from educator Ron Berger points the same way: his craftsmanship-focused classes run work through repeated drafts and structured peer critique — famously, a student's drawing of a butterfly transformed across six drafts of feedback — teaching kids that early versions of anything, including their beliefs, are drafts awaiting revision.
The Lecture Illusion: Learning More While Feeling You Learned Less

Lectures are beloved and dominant — and measurably inferior for learning. A landmark meta-analysis led by Scott Freeman covering STEM courses found students in traditional lecture classes were about 1.5 times more likely to fail than students in active-learning classes, where class time goes to problem-solving, discussion, and application; active learning also raised exam performance by roughly half a letter grade.

Why does lecturing persist? Harvard physicist Louis Deslauriers ran the decisive experiment in 2019: students were randomly assigned to identical content delivered either as a polished lecture or as an active-learning session. The active-learning students scored significantly higher on tests of the material — yet rated their own learning lower and enjoyed the class less than the lecture students. A fluent, charismatic lecture creates a feeling of understanding — cognitive ease that students mistake for mastery. Active learning feels harder and more confusing precisely because effortful struggle is what builds durable knowledge, a phenomenon psychologists call desirable difficulty.

The implication runs beyond classrooms. Any experience optimized to feel smooth — a slick presentation, a confident pundit, a frictionless narrative — can maximize the sensation of learning while minimizing the reality. Grant's warning: entertainment value and educational value are different axes, and audiences reliably confuse them. If you want people (or yourself) to actually rethink, build in friction — questions, problems, disagreement, drafts — and expect it to feel worse in the moment than it performs on the test. Deep learning, like difficult conversation, is supposed to be uncomfortable.
In practice
Before your next charged conversation, find and mention at least three distinct perspectives on the issue — not two — and name a point where your own side disagrees internally.
Add honest caveats to your next persuasive memo or post ("this holds best when...; the evidence is weaker on..."); state your confidence as a range, not a verdict.
Segment your audience before communicating on a hot topic: identify who is dismissive, who is merely doubtful or disengaged, and write for the persuadable middle instead of the extreme.
Replace perspective-taking with perspective-seeking: ask one person who disagrees with you how they came to their view, and listen for the experience behind it.
Teach or learn something this week through active struggle — solve problems before seeing solutions, critique a source before accepting it — and expect it to feel less effective than it is.
Treat one authoritative source you rely on as a draft: find what it omits or gets outdated, the way McCarthy's students interrogate their textbook.
